text: I arrive as open-source contributor deciding whether a project is worth a first PR. My past: I have wasted weekends on inactive repositories and now look for maintainers, docs, issues, and contribution signals before investing time.
Today I want to: Find whether GitHub helps me judge repository credibility and contribution paths quickly.
My life goal is: Build a public technical reputation without wasting years on dead projects.
I have 100 life-years. Each meaningful action costs 1 month(s); each committed dollar costs 1 year(s).

label: arrival
title: GitHub · Change is constant. GitHub keeps you ahead. · GitHub

text: I see "GitHub · Change is constant. GitHub keeps you ahead. · GitHub".
I think: I landed on GitHub, but this screen tells me brand promise more than whether a specific repository is alive.
My reaction: My first reaction is skeptical interest; I trust GitHub as a place to inspect projects, but the homepage itself does not answer my contributor question.
Idea: show a fast contributor credibility path from the front page, like active projects, maintainer signals, and first-PR routes.
Life-cost decision: Spending 1 month of life on one more careful move is worth it if it gets me closer to judging real project activity quickly.
Truth assessment: My honest judgment is that GitHub is probably useful for my goal, but I have low confidence from this screen alone.
